[[section-search]]
= Search & AI Embeddings

Capri DAM provides two complementary search modes:

* **Full-text search** â€” powered by PostgreSQL `tsvector` / `tsquery`.
* **Semantic search** â€” powered by **pgvector** with float embeddings generated by a configurable AI provider.

Both modes are exposed through the unified `GET /api/v1/search` endpoint and the
`search` GraphQL query.

== Full-text search

=== Indexed fields

A generated `tsvector` column combines `title`, `description`, `tags`, `custom_metadata` values,
and extracted `metadata` (EXIF/IPTC captions).

[source,sql]
----
-- Simplified representation of the search index
CREATE INDEX assets_search_idx ON assets USING gin(search_vector);
----

=== Query

[source,http]
----
GET /api/v1/search?q=product+launch&folder_id=5&content_type[]=image/jpeg
----

Rails generates:

[source,ruby]
----
Asset.where("search_vector @@ plainto_tsquery(:q)", q: "product launch")
     .where(folder_id: folder_subtree_ids)
     .where(content_type: ["image/jpeg"])
     .order("ts_rank(search_vector, plainto_tsquery(?)) DESC", "product launch")
----

=== Facets

`AssetFilterBar` (UI) and the API support multi-select filters on:
`content_type`, `tags`, `created_at` range, `status`, `folder_id`.

=== Pagination

`page`/`per_page` are supported on all three payload builders (lexical,
semantic, and folder search). `per_page` defaults to
`Api::V1::SearchController::DEFAULT_PER_PAGE` (25, matching the folder/asset
grid's `AssetFilterBar` default) and is clamped to `[1, 100]`. The `/search`
screen's results-per-page dropdown offers `25`/`50`/`100` (see
`SearchScreen.jsx`'s `PER_PAGE_OPTIONS`), defaulting to 25.

=== Result size formatting

`SearchController#format_asset` derives the human-readable `size` field from
the raw byte count stored by `AssetProcessorWorker` (`properties["size"]`,
falling back to the legacy `properties["file_size"]` key), via
`ActiveSupport::NumberHelper.number_to_human_size`. The raw byte count is also
returned separately as `file_size` for any consumer that needs it unformatted.
Earlier, `format_asset` read `properties["size_human"]`/`properties["file_size"]`
keys that `AssetProcessorWorker` never actually wrote, so every search result
always showed a hard-coded `"0 KB"`.

=== Including Recycle Bin assets

By default, `GET /api/v1/search` only returns active (non-trashed) assets
(`Asset.active`). Passing `?include_bin=true` switches the base scope to
`Asset.all` (see `SearchController#include_bin?`), so soft-deleted assets are
included alongside active ones. Every result carries an `in_bin` boolean so
the UI can flag which results are bin items â€” `SearchResultCard.jsx` renders
a "Bin" chip when `in_bin` is true. The `/search` screen exposes this as an
"Include Recycle Bin" toggle switch, off by default.

=== Result navigation (asset deep-link convention)

Every screen that lets a user click through to an individual asset (Search
results, Collections, notification `action_url`, workflow task-assignment
emails, etc.) **must** navigate to:

[source]
----
/assets?id=<uuid-or-id>
----

`GET /assets` is served by `DashboardController#assets`, which renders the
same shell as `/folders` and stamps `data-initial-target-asset-id` on the
root `<div>`. `AssetExplorer.jsx` reads that attribute, resolves the asset
(first from the already-loaded page, then via `GET /api/v1/assets/:id`, which
accepts either the numeric `id` or the `uuid`), and opens `AssetViewer`
automatically.

There is **no** `/assets/:id` route â€” building a link like
``/assets/${asset.uuid}`` will 404. This bit `SearchResultCard` navigation
until it was fixed (grid + list view `onClick` handlers in
`ExplorerTopBar`'s sibling `SearchScreen.jsx`), and the same mistake had crept
into `WorkflowActionExecutor#send_email`/`#send_in_app` and
`WorkflowMailer#task_assigned`, which built dead
`/dashboard?view=asset_explorer&asset=<id>` links (that route ignores those
params entirely). All of these now build the `/assets?id=` deep-link instead.

When adding a new place that links to an asset, always use `/assets?id=` (or
the `navigateTo` helper from `utils/globalutils.js`, which already does this
correctly for Collections) â€” never construct a `/assets/:id`-style path.

Note: `/folders?folder=<id>&id=<id>` is also a supported deep-link form (used
by e.g. the Duplicate Manager's "Go to asset" action) â€” `DashboardController
#folders` reads `params[:id]` the same way `#assets` does, and stamps the same
`data-initial-target-asset-id` attribute.

=== Asset payload shape for the deep-link fetch (`properties`, not just `metadata`)

`AssetExplorer.jsx`'s deep-link effect calls `GET /api/v1/assets/:id`
(`Api::V1::AssetsController#show`) and feeds the raw JSON straight into
`<AssetViewer asset={...} />`. `AssetViewer.jsx` (and the rest of the asset UI)
reads `asset.properties.content_type`, `asset.properties.file_size`, etc. â€”
the same shape `FoldersController#format_asset_payload` returns for assets
loaded via the normal folder listing.

`AssetsController#format_asset` previously only returned a `metadata:` key
(no `properties:`), so `asset.properties` was `undefined` for anything opened
via a deep link â€” `AssetViewer`'s `isImage` check silently evaluated to
`false` and the viewer fell back to a generic file icon instead of loading the
image, even though the dialog itself opened correctly. Fixed by adding
`properties: metadata` to the response (kept `metadata:` as a legacy alias for
any other existing consumers). When adding new fields to asset JSON payloads,
prefer `properties` as the canonical metadata key for parity with the folder
listing endpoint.

While the deep-link fetch is in flight, `AssetExplorer.jsx` shows a
full-screen `Backdrop`/`CircularProgress` spinner (`data-testid=
"deep-link-loading-backdrop"`) instead of leaving the user staring at an
unchanged folder grid.

=== Thumbnail/preview resolution for special file extensions

`SearchController#format_asset`/`#suggestion_for_asset` populate `thumb_url`
and `preview_url` via `AssetUrlHelper#asset_preview_url_for`, **not**
`#asset_url_for`. This matters for formats a browser cannot decode natively in
an `<img>` tag â€” PSD, TIFF, HEIC, RAW, PDF, AI, EPS, InDesign, and any other
MIME type outside `AssetProcessorWorker::WEB_RENDERABLE_MIME_TYPES` â€” which
`AssetProcessorWorker` flattens into a first-page/composited PNG stored at
`preview_storage_path` on ingest. `asset_preview_url_for` resolves to
`.../assets/local/:uuid?variant=preview` (or the CDN equivalent) when a
preview exists, and transparently falls back to the raw `asset_url_for` URL
otherwise, so the field is always safe to render unconditionally.

Each search result also carries:

* `url` â€” the original/raw file URL (for download or full-quality use), and
* `web_renderable` â€” `true` when `content_type` is browser-native
  (`AssetProcessorWorker::WEB_RENDERABLE_MIME_TYPES`), i.e. `thumb_url` points
  at the original file rather than a generated preview.

Before this, `format_asset` used `asset_url_for` for `thumb_url`, so any
non-web-native "special extension" (PSD/TIFF/HEIC/RAW/etc.) rendered a broken
`<img>` in `SearchResultCard`/`AssetCard`-style thumbnails on the `/search`
screen even though a perfectly good flattened preview existed and was already
used correctly on `/folders` and `/assets` (`Api::V1::AssetsController#format_asset`
already used `asset_preview_url_for`). `SearchResultCard.jsx` no longer gates
rendering on `content_type.startsWith('image/')` either â€” it renders whatever
URL the backend provides and falls back to the file-type icon only if the
`<img>` actually fails to load (`onError`), so a slow/failed preview
generation degrades gracefully instead of leaving a broken image forever.

== Global search bar (top-bar) & `/search` screen

The top-bar `GlobalSearchBar` component and the `/search` screen share the same
`GET /api/v1/search` endpoint (`Api::V1::SearchController`). The `mode` query
param selects one of five pipelines:

[cols="1,3", options="header"]
|===
| `mode`             | Pipeline
| `images` (default) | Lexical ILIKE search restricted to `image/*` content types
| `files`            | Lexical ILIKE search restricted to non-image content types
| `folders`          | Searches `Folder` records by `name` (not `Asset`) â€” see `build_folder_payload`
| `visual`           | Semantic pgvector search: embeds `q` via the AI Gateway, then `Asset.nearest_to_vector`
| `agentic`          | Same semantic pipeline as `visual` â€” surfaced as "Ask AI Agent" in the UI
|===

`visual`/`agentic` gracefully **fall back** to the lexical pipeline
(`meta.semantic_fallback: true`) whenever the AI Gateway is unreachable or
returns no embedding, so the search bar never hard-fails â€” see
`SearchController#build_semantic_payload`.

=== Autocomplete suggestions

`GET /api/v1/search/suggestions?q=...&mode=...` powers the debounced dropdown
in `GlobalSearchBar.jsx` (200ms debounce, 2-character minimum). It returns up
to 5 matching assets + 3 matching folders, each with a direct `href` â€” asset
suggestions link straight to `/assets?id=<uuid>` (see the deep-link convention
above) and folder suggestions link to `/folders?folder=<id>`. Users can also
navigate the dropdown with `â†‘`/`â†“`/`Enter`, or jump to it from anywhere via the
`âŒ˜K` / `Ctrl+K` shortcut.

=== Redis-backed caching (`SearchCache`)

Both `index` and `suggestions` responses are cached in Redis
(`app/services/search_cache.rb`) keyed by a SHA-256 hash of the sorted query
params, under the `dam:search:*` namespace:

* `index` responses â€” 30s TTL
* `suggestions` responses â€” 15s TTL

`SearchCache.fetch` is deliberately defensive: any `Redis::BaseError` (or
`SystemCallError`, e.g. connection refused) is caught and logged, and the
block runs uncached instead of raising â€” search must never go down because
Redis is unavailable. Caching is skipped entirely in the test environment
(mirrors `config.cache_store = :null_store`) since specs mutate fixtures
per-example. Call `SearchCache.flush_all` after a bulk import if you need to
force-bust every cached search result immediately.

=== Index-backed lexical search (`pg_trgm`)

The lexical pipeline's `ILIKE` queries (title/filename/full-metadata substring
matching, plus content-type prefix filters) are backed by PostgreSQL trigram
(`pg_trgm`) GIN indexes rather than running as sequential scans â€” see
migration `db/migrate/20260716113000_add_trigram_search_indexes_for_lexical_search.rb`:

[cols="1,3", options="header"]
|===
| Index | Backs
| `index_assets_on_title_trgm` | `title ILIKE '%...%'`
| `index_assets_on_properties_original_filename_trgm` | `properties->>'original_filename' ILIKE '%...%'`
| `index_assets_on_properties_text_trgm` | `properties::text ILIKE '%...%'` (the "search anywhere in metadata" fallback â€” the most expensive of the three pre-index, since it cast the whole JSONB blob to text per row)
| `index_assets_on_properties_content_type_trgm` | `properties->>'content_type' ILIKE 'image/%'` and similar prefix patterns (`apply_mode_filter`/`apply_mime_group`)
| `index_folders_on_name_trgm` | `Folder#name ILIKE '%...%'` (folder-mode search + suggestions)
|===

A trigram GIN index (`gin_trgm_ops`) was chosen over rewriting these queries
against a `tsvector` column because it accelerates `ILIKE` **without changing
matching semantics** â€” substring matches like `"dup1"` inside
`Brand_Guidelines_dup1.jpg` (exercised by
`spec/requests/api/v1/search_spec.rb`) keep working exactly as before, just
via an index scan instead of a sequential scan. Confirmed with `EXPLAIN`
(`SET enable_seqscan = off`) that Postgres produces a `Bitmap Index Scan`
plan for every `ILIKE` pattern the controller issues. A `tsvector`-based
full-text engine (with ranking/stemming) remains a candidate future upgrade
if ranked relevance scoring is ever needed â€” see ADR 3 in
link:../../architecture/index.html#section-design-decisions[the architecture design
decisions] and the corresponding entry in
link:../../architecture/index.html#section-technical-risks[technical risks].

The pre-existing `index_assets_on_properties` GIN index (`jsonb_ops`) is
unaffected and continues to serve any `@>`/`?`/`?|`/`?&` containment queries
elsewhere in the codebase â€” it does not, and cannot, accelerate `->>` text
extraction combined with `ILIKE`, which is why the dedicated trigram indexes
above were necessary.



=== Embedding generation

When an asset is processed, `ExtractionWorker` queues an embedding generation call:

[source,ruby]
----
# app/services (conceptual)
class EmbeddingService
  def generate(asset)
    text = [asset.title, asset.description, asset.metadata['caption']].compact.join(' ')
    vector = ai_client.embed(text)          # calls configured AI provider
    asset.create_asset_embedding!(vector:)  # stored as pgvector column
  end
end
----

The AI provider (OpenAI, Cohere, local Ollama, etc.) is configured in `AiConfiguration`.

=== Similarity search

[source,http]
----
POST /api/v1/search/similar
{ "asset_id": 42, "limit": 10 }
----

[source,ruby]
----
AssetEmbedding.nearest_neighbors(:vector, target.vector, distance: :cosine)
              .limit(10)
              .map(&:asset)
----

=== Semantic Collection Cluster Map

The `Collections/SemanticClusterMap.jsx` component renders an `@xyflow/react`
force-directed graph where nodes are assets and edge weights are cosine similarity
scores.  Clicking a cluster auto-selects assets for a new collection.

== Re-ranking

When `semantic: true` is passed to the search endpoint, the API:

. Runs the full-text query to get candidate IDs.
. Fetches their embeddings.
. Re-ranks results by cosine distance to the query embedding.
. Returns the merged, re-ranked list.

== AI Copilot

The AI Copilot feature (`POST /api/v1/copilots/chat`) provides a chat interface
for asset discovery:

[source,http]
----
POST /api/v1/copilots/chat
{ "message": "Show me all hero images approved last month" }
----

The copilot translates natural-language queries into filter predicates and returns
a structured result with a plain-text explanation.

Requires `AiConfiguration.active = true` and a valid API key.

== Configuration

[source,yaml]
----
# Managed via Admin â†’ Intelligence or PUT /api/v1/ai_configurations/:id
provider: openai          # openai | cohere | ollama | azure_openai
model: text-embedding-3-small
api_key: <encrypted>
dimensions: 1536
batch_size: 50
----

Re-generate all embeddings:

[source,bash]
----
# Enqueue a full-library re-embedding run
bundle exec rails runner "Asset.find_each { |a| ExtractionWorker.perform_async(a.id) }"
----

Or use the Admin â†’ Intelligence â†’ "Re-embed All Assets" button.

== Data health

`GET /api/v1/data_health` reports:

[source,json]
----
{
  "assets_without_embeddings": 12,
  "quarantined_assets": 3,
  "orphaned_blobs": 0,
  "missing_thumbnails": 5
}
----
